title: Digital Design and Computer Architecture
author: David Harris, Sarah Harris
summary: A foundational book that connects logic design, computer architecture, Verilog, state machines, and processor design into a coherent engineering framework.
verdict: This book transformed digital design from a collection of topics into a system of interconnected ideas.

details.whyRead: I wanted to build stronger foundations in digital design and understand how hardware systems are constructed from logic gates up to processors.
details.expectation: I expected a textbook focused mainly on theory and exam preparation.
details.whatFound: Instead, I found a structured explanation of how digital systems evolve from simple logic into complete computing architectures.
keyTakeaways: Digital systems are built from layers of abstraction.
Architecture matters as much as implementation.
Timing is a design requirement, not an afterthought.
details.whatChanged: After reading this book, I became significantly more interested in RTL design, FPGA development, and processor architecture.
details.favoriteQuote: The important thing is the system and not the sum of its parts.
details.recommendation: Highly recommended for students interested in digital design, FPGA development, computer architecture, or RTL engineering.

title: The Cuckoo's Egg
author: Cliff Stoll
summary: A real-world story of tracking an intruder through network logs, debugging system behavior, and learning how complex systems fail.
verdict: This book showed me that engineering is as much about curiosity and persistence as it is about technical skill.

details.whyRead: I wanted to understand how real systems are investigated when something breaks in the wild.
details.expectation: I expected an obscure history of early computing security incidents.
details.whatFound: Instead, I found a gripping narrative that connected system monitoring, human behavior, and the need to trace symptoms back to causes.
keyTakeaways: Good debugging is often slow, methodical, and based on evidence.
Complex systems hide their failures in small, subtle anomalies.
Investigation skills are as important as coding skills in system engineering.
details.whatChanged: It made me care more about observability, log analysis, and the process of following a failure from symptom to root cause.
details.favoriteQuote: The only thing more expensive than education is ignorance.
details.recommendation: Recommended for engineers and students who want a practical view of how systems are defended and diagnosed.

title: Code: The Hidden Language of Computer Hardware and Software
author: Charles Petzold
summary: A beautifully written journey through the fundamentals of computing, from switches and circuits to assembly language and operating systems.
verdict: This book deepened my understanding of the invisible machinery under every program and every piece of hardware.

details.whyRead: I wanted to see the connections between physical circuits and the software abstractions I use every day.
details.expectation: I expected a gentle introduction to computers without deep engineering detail.
details.whatFound: Instead, I found clear engineering intuition built on physical components, binary logic, and elegant examples.
keyTakeaways: Computers are built from simple physical components.
High-level software is only meaningful when mapped to hardware behavior.
Understanding abstractions starts with understanding their physical roots.
details.whatChanged: It made me a better engineer by reminding me that every abstraction is built on something concrete.
details.favoriteQuote: A computer is made of silicon, but programming is made of ideas.
details.recommendation: Great for anyone who wants a richer sense of how hardware and software connect.
